text: The laparoscopic camera enters the abdomen through the umbilical port, revealing fibrinous adhesions around the gallbladder. The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity, noting potential mild perforation of the gallbladder. The epigastric port is established by making an incision in the skin of the epigastrium.

The surgeon positions the camera close to a mass in the mid-axillary line on the right side, carefully introducing the fourth port while avoiding injury to nearby structures such as the colon and small intestine. The surgeon assesses the adhesions around the gallbladder, noting that they are flimsy rather than fibrous, and prepares to remove them.

The surgeon attempts to identify the gallbladder fundus, which is obscured by adhesions. Dissection is performed to clear the omentum from the gallbladder's fundus, gently lifting and separating tissue planes. The surgeon avoids applying force, opting for careful dissection to clarify the anatomy.

The surgeon carefully identifies the correct planes around the gallbladder, which is globular in shape and slowly being exposed. Adhesions are present between the gallbladder and liver, requiring gentle traction to separate them. The gallbladder is found to be full of pus, prompting the use of suction for aspiration.

The surgeon observes the gallbladder wall, which appears gangrenous with pus points and is very slippery. The gallbladder is filled with infected bile and mucus, which are being aspirated using a suction tip. The suction tip aids in dissection by gently pulling and minimizing trauma, revealing the gallbladder as it slowly comes into view.

The surgeon uses a funder grasper to manipulate the gallbladder, which appears rotten and full of pus. The surgical team has reached the base of the gallbladder, where they are identifying its attachments to the common bile duct and common hepatic duct. The surgeon is securing the gallbladder's blood supply by dealing with the cystic artery and clipping its blood supply, while removing some of its peritoneal coverage.

The surgeon uses a Maryland curved dissector to gently peel away the rotten peritoneal coverings from the gallbladder, aiming to expose it completely. The infundibulum holder is used to manipulate the gallbladder, allowing dissection on the right side, left side, and below the gallbladder with delicate movements.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver bed, removing surrounding necrotic tissue to prevent entry into the liver. The posterior window is being created to access the Calot's triangle, where the cystic duct and artery will be identified. The gallbladder is gradually mobilized, revealing its anatomical structures.

The surgeon has opened part of the Calot's triangle, revealing fascia due to infection. The cystic duct and cystic artery are identified, but further dissection is performed to achieve the critical view of safety. The gallbladder is mobilized to expose the lower third portion of the liver bed, ensuring clear visualization of the cystic duct, cystic artery, and common hepatic duct. The surgeon carefully dissects around soft lymph nodes and fascia, aiming to preserve vital structures connecting the liver and intestine.

The surgeon performs suction in the area, revealing the swollen and rotten gallbladder. The thickened peritoneum of the gallbladder is visible, along with the vein and artery. The common bile duct and common hepatic duct are clearly identified, with the lower portion of the fascia extending over the common hepatic duct.
The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct using a clip applicator, ensuring the tip of the applicator is visible on the other side of the duct. The clips are pressed firmly to secure the duct.
